label: Shofetim (Judges) — Part 2 — Chapters 8–14
summary_plain: After Gid'on's victory, the people ask him to rule over them — he refuses: YHWH will rule over you. But Gid'on makes a golden ephod that becomes a snare. His son Avimelekh murders seventy brothers and makes himself king at Shekhem — the first Israelite to claim kingship by force. Yotam speaks the parable of the trees. YHWH raises Yiftach as judge — he makes a rash vow. Shimshon is born — a Nazirite from the womb, whose strength comes from YHWH.
summary_scholarly: The ACR reads from 4QJudg-a, 4QJudg-b and the Orit Ge'ez. Gid'on's refusal of kingship (8:23) is the clearest statement of theocratic principle in the pre-monarchic period. Avimelekh's self-made kingship at Shekhem (ch 9) is the anti-model — human power seized without YHWH's appointment. Shimshon's Nazirite dedication (ch 13) connects to the Bamidbar 6 Nazirite laws.

key_terms.definition: Gid'on's son by a Shekhemite concubine. He murders seventy of his brothers and makes himself king at Shekhem. His kingship is self-appointed, not YHWH-appointed. He dies when a woman drops a millstone on his head.
A Nazirite from birth — set apart by YHWH before his birth. His strength is from YHWH, not from his hair. Anglicized as Samson.
A judge who defeats the Ammonites. He makes a rash vow — whatever comes out of his house first will be offered to YHWH. His daughter comes out. Anglicized as Jephthah.

fill_blank.prompt: Gid'on said to them: I will not rule over you, and my son will not rule over you. ______ will rule over you.
fill_blank.source_quote: Gid'on said to them: I will not rule over you, and my son will not rule over you. YHWH will rule over you.

fill_blank.prompt: The child shall be a ______ to The Creator from the womb, and he shall begin to save Yisra'EL from the hand of the Philistines.
fill_blank.source_quote: For behold, you shall conceive and bear a son. No razor shall come upon his head, for the child shall be a Nazirite to The Creator from the womb.

multiple_choice.question: What did Gid'on say when asked to be king?
options: YHWH will rule over you — I will not
He asked for time to decide
multiple_choice.source_quote: I will not rule over you, and my son will not rule over you. YHWH will rule over you.

multiple_choice.question: How did Avimelekh become king?
options: He murdered seventy brothers and seized power
multiple_choice.source_quote: Avimelekh went to his mother's relatives at Shekhem — and they gave him seventy pieces of silver. He hired worthless and reckless fellows and killed his seventy brothers.

faq.question: What does Gid'on's refusal of kingship mean?
faq.answer: Gid'on says: 'I will not rule over you, and my son will not rule over you. YHWH will rule over you' (8:23). This is the clearest statement of theocratic principle in the pre-monarchic period. YHWH is the direct king. Human monarchy is not the covenant design. When the people later demand a king in 1 Shemu'el 8, YHWH tells Shemu'el: they have rejected me from being king over them. The Qumran community returned to this pre-monarchic framework — no king, no institutional hierarchy, just the covenant texts and YHWH's direct governance.

faq.question: What does Avimelekh's kingship reveal?
faq.answer: Avimelekh seizes power through murder — killing seventy brothers on a single stone (9:5). His kingship is the anti-model: human power seized without YHWH's appointment, through violence, funded by the temple of Baal-Berith (9:4). He dies when a woman drops a millstone on his head (9:53). The text's message: self-appointed power, seized through violence and funded by false worship, ends in destruction. Every empire documented in the Great Eraser research — Roman, British, Portuguese, Belgian — followed the same pattern: power seized through violence, maintained through institutional systems, and destined for judgment.
